chapterSummary: Gatsby's parties cease, revealing his singular focus on Daisy. A sweltering afternoon at the Buchanan's escalates into a tense confrontation in New York, where Tom exposes Gatsby's illicit past. The chapter culminates in a tragic car accident involving Myrtle Wilson, with Daisy at the wheel, and Gatsby's desperate attempt to protect her, further entangling their fates.

characters.name: Nick Carraway
characters.description: Observes the escalating tensions and moral decay, becoming increasingly disillusioned and isolated.

knowledge.content: Gatsby has fired all his original servants and replaced them with people connected to Wolfshiem to prevent gossip about Daisy's frequent visits.
The new servants at Gatsby's mansion are brothers and sisters who used to run a small hotel, arranged by Wolfshiem.
Daisy's voice is 'full of money,' revealing the source of her allure and the deep-seated class distinctions.
He realizes it is his thirtieth birthday, marking a significant personal milestone amidst the chaos.
Myrtle Wilson has been killed in a car accident.
Daisy was driving the car that killed Myrtle Wilson, and Gatsby intends to take the blame to protect her.
Tom and Daisy are conspiring together after the accident, showing a natural intimacy despite their unhappiness.

characters.name: Jay Gatsby
characters.description: His romantic obsession with Daisy drives him to drastic measures, culminating in a desperate attempt to protect her after a tragic accident.

knowledge.content: He has replaced his servants with Wolfshiem's people to ensure discretion regarding Daisy's visits.
He believes Daisy's reaction to his party and her presence at his house is paramount, leading him to dismiss his staff.
Tom Buchanan is suspicious of him and his 'old sport' affectation.
He admits he only attended Oxford for five months after the armistice, as part of a program for officers.
He reveals his 'drugstore' business was a front for selling grain alcohol during Prohibition, confirming Tom's suspicions.
He left Walter Chase to go to jail for a month in New Jersey, indicating a ruthless side to his business dealings.
He is willing to take the blame for Myrtle's death to protect Daisy, demonstrating the depth of his devotion.
He believes Tom will try to 'bother' Daisy about the 'unpleasantness' of the afternoon, showing his distrust of Tom.

characters.name: Tom Buchanan
characters.description: His possessiveness and class prejudice ignite a brutal confrontation with Gatsby, and he later manipulates the situation after Myrtle's death.

knowledge.content: He suspects Gatsby is a bootlegger and is determined to expose him.
He realizes Daisy and Gatsby are having an affair, confirmed by Daisy's declaration of love.
His belief that 'intermarriage between black and white' is a sign of societal decay, revealing his deeply ingrained racism.
He reveals Gatsby's 'drugstore' business was a front for bootlegging grain alcohol.
He knows Gatsby left Walter Chase to go to jail for a month over their bootlegging operation.
Myrtle Wilson has been killed in a car accident.
He quickly deduces that Gatsby's yellow car was involved in the accident and attempts to deflect suspicion from himself and Daisy.
He lies to George Wilson, claiming the yellow car he was driving was not his, and that he hasn't seen it all afternoon.
He observes Daisy and Gatsby's intimacy, leading to a confrontation.

characters.name: Daisy Buchanan
characters.description: Caught between Gatsby and Tom, she reveals her conflicted loyalties and ultimately causes a tragic accident.

knowledge.content: She openly declares her love for Gatsby in front of Tom.
She expresses her deep unhappiness and confusion with her life and marriage.
She is unaware of the full extent of Gatsby's illicit business dealings until Tom reveals them.
She admits to loving both Tom and Gatsby at different times, unable to fully commit to Gatsby's idealized past.
She was driving the car that killed Myrtle Wilson.
She participates in the cover-up of the accident, allowing Gatsby to take the blame.

characters.name: Jordan Baker
characters.description: Observes the unfolding drama with cynical detachment, yet her presence offers Nick some comfort.

knowledge.content: She hears the rumor that Tom is on the phone with his mistress.
She recognizes the social tension and attempts to lighten the mood with cynical remarks.
She is mistaken by Myrtle Wilson for Tom's wife, leading to Myrtle's jealous outburst.

characters.name: Unfamiliar Butler
characters.description: A new, rude servant at Gatsby's mansion.

characters.name: Finn
characters.description: Nick's servant, who provides information about Gatsby's new staff.

knowledge.content: Gatsby dismissed all his old servants and replaced them with new ones who wouldn't gossip.

characters.name: Grocery Boy
characters.description: A local delivery boy who observes the state of Gatsby's kitchen.

knowledge.content: Gatsby's kitchen is a 'pigsty,' indicating the new servants' lack of domestic care.

characters.name: Woman on Train
characters.description: A fellow passenger on Nick's train, suffering from the oppressive heat.

characters.name: Conductor
characters.description: A train conductor who comments on the heat.

characters.name: Pammy Buchanan
characters.description: Daisy and Tom's daughter, briefly presented to Gatsby.

characters.name: Nurse
characters.description: Pammy's attendant.

characters.name: Biloxi
characters.description: A mysterious man who attended Tom and Daisy's wedding.

characters.name: Waiter
characters.description: Serves mint and ice at the Plaza Hotel suite.

characters.name: Walter Chase
characters.description: An associate of Gatsby and Wolfshiem who was left to go to jail.

characters.name: Michaelis
characters.description: A young Greek who runs the coffee joint near Wilson's garage and is a witness to the accident.

knowledge.content: George Wilson is physically ill and agitated, having discovered his wife's infidelity.
Myrtle Wilson has been killed by a car.
He heard Myrtle Wilson arguing with George just before the accident.
The car that hit Myrtle was light green, though he is unsure.

characters.name: Policeman
characters.description: Investigates the accident at Wilson's garage.

knowledge.content: Myrtle Wilson was instantly killed by a hit-and-run driver.
The car involved was yellow.

characters.name: Well-dressed Negro
characters.description: A witness who saw the yellow car speeding.

knowledge.content: The car that hit Myrtle was a big, new yellow car, speeding.

characters.name: Doctor
characters.description: Called to the scene of the accident.

characters.name: George B. Wilson
characters.description: Discovers his wife's infidelity, becomes physically ill, and is devastated by her death.

knowledge.content: He has discovered his wife, Myrtle, has a life apart from him, leading to his physical sickness and decision to move West.
He intends to take Myrtle West, whether she wants to or not, to escape her infidelity.
His wife, Myrtle, has been killed in a car accident.
He knows the car that killed Myrtle was yellow.

characters.name: Myrtle Wilson
characters.description: Mistakes Jordan Baker for Daisy, leading to a jealous outburst, and is tragically killed in a hit-and-run accident.

knowledge.content: She mistakes Jordan Baker for Tom's wife, Daisy, fueling her jealousy.

locations.name: Gatsby's Mansion
locations.description: Now quiet and less frequented, reflecting Gatsby's singular focus on Daisy.
lore: The parties have ceased, and the house is now used primarily for Daisy's visits.
The kitchen is described as a 'pigsty' by the grocery boy, indicating a shift in household management.

locations.name: Buchanan Mansion
locations.description: A stifling, hot environment where tensions simmer beneath a veneer of casual elegance.
lore: The house is filled with the oppressive heat of summer, mirroring the emotional tension within.

locations.name: Salon (Buchanan Mansion)
locations.description: A dark and cool room, shadowed by awnings, where Daisy and Jordan recline like 'silver idols'.

locations.name: Veranda (Buchanan Mansion)
locations.description: An outdoor space overlooking the green Sound, where Tom and Gatsby have a tense exchange.

locations.name: Dining-room (Buchanan Mansion)
locations.description: Darkened against the heat, where the group has a nervous luncheon.

locations.name: Plaza Hotel
locations.description: A grand hotel in New York City.

locations.name: Parlour (Plaza Hotel)
locations.description: A stifling suite where the group seeks refuge from the heat and the confrontation between Tom and Gatsby erupts.
lore: The room is hot and uncomfortable, reflecting the escalating tension.

locations.name: Ballroom (Plaza Hotel)
locations.description: Located beneath the parlour, playing Mendelssohn's Wedding March, adding ironic commentary to the scene.

locations.name: George B. Wilson's Garage
locations.description: A desolate and now tragic location, where Myrtle Wilson is killed.
lore: The site of Myrtle Wilson's death, marking a turning point in the narrative.

locations.name: Office (Wilson's Garage)
locations.description: Where George Wilson is found sick and later where he is taken after Myrtle's death.

threads.description: Nick's disillusionment with the East and its moral landscape
threads.development: Nick's disillusionment deepens as he witnesses the brutal confrontation between Tom and Gatsby, the tragic death of Myrtle, and the subsequent cover-up, leading him to feel isolated and disgusted by the moral bankruptcy of those around him.

threads.description: The mystery surrounding Jay Gatsby and his true nature
threads.development: Gatsby's true nature is partially revealed as Tom exposes his bootlegging past, fracturing the carefully constructed facade. His desperate attempt to protect Daisy, even taking the blame for a fatal accident, further defines his character as driven by singular romantic obsession rather than pure criminality.

threads.description: The crumbling marriage and moral decay of Tom and Daisy Buchanan
threads.development: The marriage fractures under the weight of Daisy's affair with Gatsby and Tom's possessive rage. While they appear to reconcile in a conspiratorial intimacy after Myrtle's death, their moral decay is evident in their willingness to let Gatsby take the blame.

threads.description: The social and moral contrast between East Egg and West Egg
threads.development: The contrast intensifies as the 'new money' Gatsby's illicit past is exposed by the 'old money' Tom, highlighting the hypocrisy and moral rot present in both worlds, particularly in their reactions to Myrtle's death.

threads.description: The destructive nature of Tom Buchanan's affair with Myrtle Wilson
threads.development: The affair reaches its tragic climax with Myrtle's death, directly caused by Daisy driving Gatsby's car. George Wilson's discovery of the affair and his subsequent grief and rage set the stage for further destructive consequences.

threads.description: Nick's evolving relationship and moral judgment of Jordan Baker
threads.development: Nick's relationship with Jordan is strained by the day's events; her cynical detachment and his growing disillusionment create a rift, though she still offers him a fleeting sense of comfort.

threads.description: Gatsby's singular romantic obsession with Daisy Buchanan
threads.development: Gatsby's obsession reaches a fever pitch as he desperately tries to force Daisy to declare her love for him and abandon Tom. His willingness to take the blame for Myrtle's death underscores the all-consuming nature of his love, even as Daisy's wavering commitment threatens to shatter his illusion.

threads.description: The tragic consequences of reckless behavior and moral irresponsibility
threads.development: The casual recklessness of the wealthy characters, particularly Daisy's driving, directly leads to Myrtle Wilson's death, bringing the latent theme of destructive irresponsibility to the forefront.

scenes.summary: Curiosity about Gatsby peaks as his lavish parties abruptly cease. Nick investigates, encountering a rude new butler, and learns from his Finn that Gatsby has replaced his entire staff to prevent gossip about Daisy's frequent afternoon visits. Gatsby calls Nick, confirming the change and inviting him to lunch at Daisy's, setting the stage for a tense encounter.

scenes.summary: On a sweltering day, Nick and Gatsby arrive at the Buchanan mansion for lunch. Daisy and Gatsby share a charged, flirtatious moment, openly kissing, which Jordan observes with cynical amusement. Tom, suspicious and agitated, makes a phone call that Jordan speculates is to his mistress. The brief appearance of Pammy, Daisy's daughter, momentarily disrupts the tension, but the underlying conflict between Tom and Gatsby over Daisy is palpable, culminating in Tom's forced invitation to go to town.

scenes.summary: Seeking refuge from the heat in a Plaza Hotel suite, the simmering tensions explode into a brutal confrontation. Tom, enraged by Daisy's declaration of love for Gatsby, challenges Gatsby's claims of being an Oxford man. Gatsby, in turn, forces Daisy to admit she never loved Tom, but she falters, confessing she loved both men. Tom then exposes Gatsby's bootlegging past, shattering Gatsby's carefully constructed image and leaving Daisy terrified and withdrawn.

scenes.summary: As Tom, Nick, and Jordan drive back from New York, they stumble upon a horrific accident at Wilson's garage. Myrtle Wilson has been struck and killed by a car. George Wilson, distraught and physically ill from discovering his wife's infidelity, is inconsolable. Tom, realizing Gatsby's yellow car was involved, quickly manipulates the situation, lying to Wilson and the police to deflect suspicion from himself and Daisy, who was driving Gatsby's car.

scenes.summary: After the horrific accident, Tom, Nick, and Jordan return to the Buchanan mansion. Nick, feeling sick and disillusioned, declines to go inside, sensing a growing distance from Jordan. He discovers Gatsby standing vigil outside the house, determined to protect Daisy from Tom's potential brutality. Nick then observes Tom and Daisy in the kitchen, sharing a conspiratorial meal, an image of intimate understanding that suggests they are united in their deception, leaving Gatsby to watch over a lost cause.
